purpose: Source of truth for IRON's science-backed volume guidance. Plan Analysis and the Muscles-tab volume insight read bands from this file so every number is traceable to a source, not hardcoded. Bands are per FINE muscle (matching exerciseMuscleMap.json's 17-muscle taxonomy).
countingBasis: Bands are expressed in FRACTIONAL-TOTAL weekly sets — the same unit IRON counts (primary 1.0 + secondary 0.5). The indirect/secondary credit from compounds is already included in both the count and the band. A muscle reaching its band largely through indirect credit (e.g. biceps from rows/pulldowns) is the intended pattern, not a deficiency. NOTE: IRON currently counts WORKING sets (non-warmup), not RIR-verified hard sets — a slight overcount vs the literature's 'sets near failure'. Label the metric 'working sets' and frame guidance as 'associated with', not guaranteed.
methodology: Major-mover ranges are anchored to peer-reviewed dose-response work (Schoenfeld/Ogborn/Krieger 2017; Pelland et al. 2024 fractional meta-regression). Smaller/indirect-heavy muscle tiers and frequency/per-session guidance are practitioner consensus extrapolated from that literature (lower certainty — see evidenceTier per muscle). The 0.5 secondary weight is a modeling convention, not a measured activation fraction.
globalNotes: These are population landmarks with large individual variation. Treat as guidance, not targets to chase.
Start at the LOW end of a band and add volume only when progress stalls — not as a starting point.
Train each muscle ~2x/week; splitting weekly volume across sessions beats cramming it into one.
Volume guidance applies to a HYPERTROPHY goal. For STRENGTH, see the `strength` block — volume matters less and is lift-specific.
Where IRON has the user's own progress signal (projection/plateau engine), prefer it: below-range but still gaining = soft note; below-range and stalled = act.
maintenanceNote: ~6 fractional sets/week generally maintains a muscle even if below the growth band (useful during cuts or busy phases).

frequency.note: Most muscles: aim 2x/week. Small, fast-recovering muscles (biceps, triceps, calves, abs, forearms) tolerate 2–4x. Flag any muscle trained only 1x/week if its weekly sets are in/above the productive range (better split across days).

perSessionCeiling.note: Per muscle per session: ~10 hard sets is a practical soft ceiling; flag >16 in one session as likely junk volume / poor distribution. Suggest redistributing across days rather than adding more on the same day.

Lats.note: Major back mover; rows + pulldowns + pull-ups.

Hamstrings.note: Hinges + curls; treated as a major mover alongside quads.

Glutes.note: Large indirect credit from squats/hinges/lunges; direct work (thrusts/bridges) adds on top.

Side Delts.note: Mostly direct (raises); many lifters respond to the higher end.

Front Delts.note: Heavy indirect from all pressing — rarely needs much direct work.

Rear Delts.note: Commonly undertrained; tolerates frequent low-load work.

Biceps.note: Large indirect credit from pulls; can handle high volume / frequency.

Triceps.note: Indirect from all pressing; direct extensions add the rest.

Traps.note: Indirect from deadlifts/rows; direct shrugs as needed.

Calves.note: Stubborn; tolerate higher volume and frequency, higher rep ranges.

Abs.note: Direct flexion + anti-extension/rotation; bracing in compounds is indirect.

Forearms.note: Mostly indirect from grip; thin direct-volume evidence — don't over-flag.

Lower Back.note: Substantial indirect from hinges/squats/carries; high fatigue cost — rarely programmed high directly.

Adductors.note: Indirect from squats/lunges; thin direct evidence — guidance is approximate.

strength.applies: When the user's goal is strength (not hypertrophy).
strength.guidance: Strength is LIFT-specific, not muscle-specific — per-muscle volume matters far less than for hypertrophy. Lower total volume suffices and shows sharper diminishing returns; FREQUENCY of the trained lift is the bigger lever.
strength.note: For strength goals, emphasize: train the key lifts 2–3x/week, keep most sets heavy and a rep or two from failure, and don't over-index on per-muscle set totals. Use the volume bands above only as a soft floor (don't let a trained muscle fall to maintenance).

citations.ref: Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass. J Sports Sci. 2017.
citations.supports: Graded dose-response; 10+ weekly sets > 5–9 > 1–4 for hypertrophy.

citations.ref: Pelland JC, Robinson ZP, et al. Resistance training volume dose-response meta-regression (fractional set method). 2024.
citations.supports: Fractional counting (0.5 indirect); diminishing returns; gains continue past 20 with much-reduced per-set effect; strength diminishes faster and is frequency-sensitive.

citations.ref: Coaching consensus syntheses of the above (e.g. major 10–20 / smaller 6–10 direct tiering; calves & abs 2–5 direct sets, 2–4x/week; arms 3–4 sets 2x/week).
citations.supports: Per-muscle tiering for smaller/indirect-heavy muscles, frequency and per-session ceilings.

honestyCaveats: Working-set proxy: IRON counts non-warmup sets, not RIR-verified hard sets — bands assume sets taken ~0–3 reps from failure. Slight overcount; frame as 'working sets'.
0.5 secondary factor is a modeling convention from the fractional method, not a measured value.
Low-evidence muscles (forearms, lower back, adductors) have approximate bands — surface them with less assertive copy and never as a hard 'deficiency'.
Strength bands are coarse and lift-driven; do not present per-muscle strength volume with false precision.
